pages.text: Assessment of strength development in blended cement admixed Bangkok clay Suksun Horpibulsuk a,⇑, Runglawan Rachan b, Apichat Suddeepong c a School of Civil Engineering, Construction Technology Research Unit, Suranaree University of Technology, 111 University Avenue, Muang District, N akhon Ratchasima 30000, Thailand b Department of Civil Engineering, Mahanakorn University of Technology, 51 Cheum-Sampan Rd., Nong Chok, Bangkok 10530, Thailand c School of Civil Engineering, Suranaree University of Technology, 111 University Avenue, Muang District, Nakhon Ratchasima 30000, Thailand article info Article history: Received 19 April 2010 Received in revised form 2 August 2010 Accepted 23 August 2010 Available online 20 September 2010 Keywords: Abrams’ law Bangkok clay Blended cement Biomass ash Cementitious products Clay–water/cement ratio Dispersing material Equivalent cement content Fly ash Unconﬁned compressive strength abstract Fly ash and biomass ash have been widely accepted as waste materials substituting Portland cement. In this paper, the role of these two ashes on the strength development of cement admixed low-swelling Bangkok clay is investigated via unconﬁned compressive (UC) test and thermal gravity (TG) analysis. Fly ash and biomass ash are dispersing materials, increasing the reactive surface of the cement grains. The pozzolanic reaction does not play any signiﬁcant role on the strength development with time since the amount of Ca(OH) 2 is insufﬁcient to react with the ashes. The contribution of the dispersing effect to the strength development is regarded akin as an addition of cement. Based on this premise, the clay– water/cement ratio hypothesis for blended cement admixed clay is proposed for analyzing and assessing the strength development. Even with the difference in water content, cement content and ash content, the blended cement admixed clay samples having the same clay–water/cement ratio, w c/C possess prac- tically the same stress–strain response and strength. The relationship among strength, clay–water/ cement ratio, and curing time for the blended cement admixed Bangkok clay is ﬁnally developed and ver- iﬁed. It is useful to assess the strength at any curing time wherein water content, cement content, and ash content vary over a wide range by using the test result of a single laboratory trial. For the economic mix design (the most effective dispersing effect), an addition of 25% ash is recommended. It can save on the input of cement up to 15.8%. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Bangkok clay is one of the well-known soft clay deposits. It pos- sesses high water content close to its liquid limit with large poten- tial for settlement and low inherent shear strength. This clay is classiﬁed as non- to low swelling whose swelling potential in- creases with depth [1]. Besides Bangkok clay, non- to low swelling soils are generally found in many lowlands such as Ariake, Pusan, Singapore, Drammen, Louiseville clays [2–4]. Even though clay mineralogy of many clayey soils is primarily montmorillonite, montmorillonite in those soils might not be a dominant parameter, controlling the soil expansivity. The other parameters such as other clay minerals (kaolinite, illite, etc.), non-clay fraction (>0.002 mm) and pore medium chemistry can also play a great role, masking the role of montmorillonite. As such, it is possible that some clayey soils can be classiﬁed as non-swelling or low swelling type, even th

the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) standard. The rate of vertical displacement in UC tests was 1 mm/min. To develop the phenomenological model, the role of wc/C on the growth of cementitious products must be understood as well as the capability of the wc/C as a prime parameter in analyzing the stress–strain response and strength development must be proved. The growth of the cementitious products is examined by the ther- mal gravity (TG) analysis. The TG analysis was performed on the FA blended cement admixed samples with wc/C values of 4 and 12. The blended cement admixed samples were broken from the cen- ter into small fragments. The samples were frozen at /C0 195 /C176Cb y immersion in liquid nitrogen for 5 min and evacuated at a pressure of 0.5 Pa at /C0 40 /C176C for 5 days [19,22]. Prior to TG testing, the dried samples were ground in a ball mill and sieved through 100 mesh (150 lm). Approximately 10–20 mg of the sample was taken for the analysis. The heat rate was maintained at 10 /C176C/min and the sample was heated up to 1000 /C176C. The amount of Ca(OH) 2 content was determined based on the weight loss between 450 and 580 /C176C [27,28] and expressed as a percentage by weight of ignited sample. When heating the samples at temperature between 450 and 580 /C176C, Ca(OH)2 is decomposed into calcium oxide (CaO) and water [25–28] as in Eq. (1). CaðOHÞ2 ! CaO þ H2O ð1Þ Due to the heat, the water is lost, leading to the decrease in overall weight. The amount of Ca(OH) 2 can be approximated from this lost water by Eq. (1), which is 4.11 times the amount of lost water [26,27]. At least three samples under the same condition were tested. As acceptance criteria, it was stipulated that the stan- dard deviation of the measured Ca(OH) 2 must be less than 10% of the mean value . It is thus ensured that the measured amount of Ca(OH)2 has a high accuracy. The TG result is widely accepted in research and development for cement industries. The growth of the cementitious products (CSH, CAH, and CASH) with the binder (cement + FA and cement + BA) contents can be expressed by the change of Ca(OH) 2 since the cementitious products and Ca(OH) 2 are the hydration products. Horpibulsuk et al. [19,22] have suc- cessfully used this technique to approximate the Ca(OH) 2 of the compacted cement stabilized clay for explaining the growth of cementitious products and strength development. The oedometer and unconﬁned compression (UC) tests on the samples having the same wc/C were carried out to prove the capa- bility of the wc/C as a prime parameter in analyzing the stress– strain response and strength development. The wc/C values of 12 and of 4, 7, and 12 at 28 days of curing were considered for the oedometer and UC tests, respectively. For oedeometer test, the clay–cement–ﬂy ash mixture was transferred to oedemeter ring, taking care to prevent any air entrapment. The oedometer samples along with the rings were wrapped in vinyl bags and cured in a humidity room of constant temperature (20 ± 2 /C176C). The test was carried out, conforming to the ASTM standard. Finally, all the strength test results were analyzed to develop a rational model for assessing the strength development in the blended cement admixed Bangkok clay based on the clay–water/ cement ratio. The proposed model was veriﬁed by the separate test results of the BA blended cement admixed Bangkok clay for differ- ent water contents ( wc = 89–148%), cement contents ( Ci = 10–30%) an

Horpibulsuk et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1521–1531 1531

Based on this parameter and Abrams’ law [14], Horpibulsuk et al. [15] have introduced a phenomenological model for predicting lab- oratory strength development in cement admixed clays at various water contents, cement contents, and curing times. This model has been reﬁned to develop a generalized strength equation for cement admixed non- to low swelling clays [6]. To reduce the construction cost of the DM method, the replace- ment of the cement by waste materials such as ﬂy ash and biomass ash is one of the best alternative ways. In Thailand, the generation of these waste materials is general far in excess of their utilization. A feasibility study of utilizing these ashes (waste materials) to par- tially replace Type I Portland cement in DM method is thus inter- esting. Fly ash has been long known as a waste pozzolanic material for concrete structure. When the pore water of the aggre- gates encounters with cement, the cement hydration occurs rap- idly. The major hydration products are hydrated calcium silicates (CSH), hydrated calcium aluminates (CAH), hydrated calcium alu- minium silicates (CASH) and hydrated lime (Ca(OH) 2). This hydra- tion leads to a rise of pH value of the pore water, which is caused by the dissociation of Ca(OH) 2. The strong bases dissolve the silica and alumina from the pozzolanic materials, in a manner similar to the reaction between a weak acid and strong base. The hydrous sil- ica and alumina will then gradually react with the calcium ions lib- erated from the hydrolysis of cement from insoluble compounds (secondary cementitious products), which hardens with time. Application of ﬂy ash for geotechnical works has been reported by many researchers [16–18]. The role of ﬂy ash on the strength development in the blended cement admixed clay has been inves- tigated both from micro- and micro observations [19,20]. Unlike in concrete (with required 28-day strength generally higher than 28 MPa), ﬂy ash in cement admixed clays (required 28-day strength only 0.6–3 MPa) does not act as the pozzolanic material because of the low Ca(OH) 2 from hydration to be reactive with. Fly ash disperses large clay–cement clusters formed due to physi- cochemical interaction into smaller clusters. The dispersion leads to the increase in the reactive surface, and hence strength enhance- ment. Horpibulsuk et al. [19] have demonstrated that particle size of the ﬂy ash insigniﬁcantly inﬂuences the dispersing effect. The present paper attempts to analyze and assess the laboratory strength development in the blended cement admixed Bangkok clay. Two blended cements were used in this study, which are the ﬂy ash blended cement and the biomass ash blended cement. The role of both the ashes on the cementitious products and the strength development is illustrated. The growth of cementitious products is examined by the thermal gravity analysis. The method of assessing the strength development is ﬁnally developed and ver- iﬁed. The clay–water/content ratio hypothesis [11–13] is extended for this development. This method is possibly applied to the other cement admixed non- to low swelling clays for different construc- tion works. 2. Clay–water/cement ratio hypothesis for blended cement admixed clay Cement, ash, and soil are particulate materials, which are com- posed of individual units. The particulate materials can be regarded as either non-interacting or interacting materials, dependent upon the absence or presence of physicochemical interactions wi

given in Table 1. Both FA and BA were passed through sieve No. 325 to remove larger particles. Total amount of the major components SiO2,A l2O3, and Fe 2O3 in FA and BA are 79.44% and 75.57%, respec- tively. FA is classiﬁed as class F ﬂy ash in accordance with ASTM C 618 whereas BA is referred to as ‘‘off-speciﬁcation” because it meets neither the class C nor class F criteria. The median particle sizes ( D 50) of FA and BA are 0.009 mm (9 lm) and 0.028 (28 lm), respectively. Grain size distribution curves of PC, FA, and BA are also shown in Fig. 1 . These curves were obtained from the laser particle size analysis. It is found that grain size distribution curves of PC and FA are similar. D50 of PC is 0.01 mm (10 lm) being almost the same as that of FA. Speciﬁc gravities of PC, FA and BA are 3.15, 2.54, and 1.95, respectively. SEM photos of PC, FA and BA are also shown in Fig. 2 . From the grain size distribution curves and the SEM photos, it is found that the particles of Bangkok clay are much smaller than those of PC, FA, and BA and the BA particles are the biggest. The PC and BA particles are irregular in shape whereas the FA particles are spherical. Bangkok clay shows groups of cluster. 3.3. Methodology The aim of this investigation is to illustrate the role of ash con- tent on the strength development of blended cement admixed Bangkok clay and to develop a phenomenological model for assess- ing its strength development. The model was developed based on the clay–water/cement ratio hypothesis. For the ﬁrst aim, Bangkok clay was passed through a 2-mm sieve to remove coarser particles. The water content was adjusted to the range of liquidity indices (LI) i.e., 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0. The liquidity index has been used in this investigation as an indicator to refer the initial water content of the clay in relation to plasticity characteristics of the clay before cement is admixed as has been done by Horpibulsuk et al. [6,12,13,15]. This intentional increase in water content is to simu- late water content increase, taking place in wet method of dispens- ing cement admixture in deep mixing. The clay with its water content, corresponding to the above levels of LI is thoroughly mixed with the blended cements (PC + FA and PC + BA) by a labo- ratory mixing machine. Cement content, C i, varies from 0% to 30%, which is commonly used for the deep mixing in soft Bangkok clay. FA and BA contents, a, vary from 0% to 60% by weight of ce- ment. The mixing time was arbitrarily ﬁxed at 10 min as recom- mended by Miura et al. [12]; and Horpibulsuk et al. [13]. Such a uniform paste was transferred to cylindrical containers of 50 mm diameter and 100 mm height, taking care to prevent any air entrapment. This sample preparation is commonly done for the ce- ment admixed soft clay by many researchers [3,6,7–13,19,20,22] etc. Since the tested clay water content is high, the cement ad- mixed clay samples become an engineering material when harden without any compaction energy. In practice, the deep mixing col- umn gets hardened and acts as a semi-rigid pile within a short per- iod after the execution (mixing of the in situ soft clay with cement slurry by a mixing blade). After 24 h, the cylindrical samples were dismantled. All the cylindrical samples were wrapped in vinyl bags and stored in a humidity room of constant temperature (20 ± 2 /C176C) until lapse of different curing times as planned. Unconﬁned com- pression (UC) tests were run on samples after 7, 14, 28, 60,

same unit (kPa, ton/m 2, psi, etc.). The B and k are constants without unit. Their values do not change with the unit of qu. The three con- stants A, B, and k for different curing times can be determined from a multi-regression analysis (MRA). When a = 0% (no ash), Eq. (2b) becomes that developed for the cement admixed Bangkok clay by Horpibulsuk et al. [6]. From the MRA results, the generalized two-dimensional qu /C0 wc/C plot is presented in Figs. 5 and 6 for the FA and BA blended cement admixed Bangkok clay, respectively. For the same curing time, the values of A, B, and k are practically identical for both the blended FA and BA admixed Bangkok clay. This implies that for a uniform mixing of clay and cement as done in the labo- ratory, the strength development in the blended cement admixed clay is independent of the chemical composition, shape and grain size of the ﬂy ash. However, the spherical shape (ﬂy ash) might provide higher strength than irregular shape (biomass ash) for non-uniformity in mixing, especially for the in situ deep mixing [5,6]. The variation of parameter A is marked and is dependent on curing time. The variation of parameter B is only between 1.25 and 1.29, and irrespective of curing time. These B-values are a typical range of the cement admixed low swelling clays [6]. The parameter k is insigniﬁcantly changed with curing time, vary- ing between 0.72 and 0.79 for both the FA and BA blended cement admixed clay. This is because the pozzolanic reaction is minimal. On the contrary, the k-value of the ﬂy ash concrete increases with curing time [23]. Since the ashes are blended in proportion to the cement content, the k-value is practically constant with cement content. It is of interest to mention that the k-value irrelevantly al- ters with ash contents, being equal to 0.75. This constant k-value implies that the dispersing effect increases linearly with ash con- tent. The higher the ash content, the greater the dispersing effect (the higher the C e). 5. Analysis and discussion The dispersing effect on the strength development is illustrated by the growth of cementitious products as shown in Table 2 .I t shows the amount of Ca(OH) 2 for the FA blended cement admixed clay at 119% water and 20% cement for different ﬂy ash contents. It is found that for the range of ash content tested ( a 6 40%), the Fig. 4. Strength development with ﬂy ash content for different cement contents. Fig. 5. Analysis of strength development in the FA blended cement admixed Bangkok clay using the clay–water/cement ratio hypothesis. Fig. 6. Analysis of strength development in the BA blended cement admixed Bangkok clay using the clay–water/cement ratio hypothesis. Table 2 Ca(OH) 2 of the FA blended cement admixed Bangkok clay for different ﬂy ash contents. wc Ci (%) a (%) Ca(OH) 2 (%) 119% 20 0 14.5 ± 0.85 10 15.4 ± 0.86 25 16.6 ± 0.94 30 16.1 ± 0.75 40 15.1 ± 0.78 S. amount of Ca(OH) 2 for all the blended cement admixed samples is higher than that of the cement admixed sample (without ﬂy ash). This higher amount of Ca(OH) 2 is associated with the higher unit weight and strength for the blended cement admixed clay. For a 6 25%, the amount of Ca(OH) 2, unit weight, and the strength in- crease with the ash content. But they tend to decrease when a > 25%. For ﬂy ash concrete, the strength increase due to pozzolanic reaction is associated with the reduction in Ca(OH) 2 [29–32].I n contrast, Table 2 shows that for a 6 25%, the strength increases with the Ca(OH) 2. This result leads to the conclusion that the poz- zolanic reaction is minimal for the blended cement admixed clay. The contribution of the ash to the strength development is thus mainly dispersing effect. This dispersing effect accelerates the ce- ment hydration. The adsorption of Ca 2+ ions from the cement hydration onto the clay particle surface leads to a decrease in repulsion between successive diffused double layers and more edge-to-face contacts between successive clay sheets [19,20,22]. Thus, clay particles ﬂocculate into larger clusters, leading to an in- crease in unit weight. The ﬂy ashes in excess of 25% possibly sur- round the cement grains and obstruct the interaction between water and cement grains. Consequently, the cementitious products decrease and hence the reduction in unit weight and strength when a > 25%. The highest amount of Ca(OH) 2 at a = 25% indicates the most effective dispersing effect. The role of the wc/C on the strength development of the blended cement admixed clay is illustrated by the growth of the cementi- tious products ( vide Table 3 ). Horpibulsuk et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1521–1531

admixed with different cement contents and ﬂy ash contents to at- tain the same wc/C values of 4 and 12. When the amount of cement is decreased, the ﬂy ash content is increased to have the same wc/C values. The k-value of 0.75 was taken for the determination of the mix proportion. The TG analysis shows that the amount of Ca(OH) 2 is practically same for the samples with the same wc/C even with different cement contents and ﬂy ash contents. In other words, the cementitious products are practically the same as long as the wc/C is the same. Table 4 shows the unit weight, strength and yield stress of the blended cement admixed Bangkok clay, having the same wc/C val- ues of 4, 7, and 12. It is found that for the same wc/C value, the unit weight of the samples is practically the same for the same initial clay water content but decreases with the initial clay water con- tent. This leads to the conclusion that for the same initial clay water content and w c/C value, with the decrease in cement content, cement hydration is accelerated by the presence of ﬂy ash, hence practically the same unit weight and strength. Even with different initial clay water content and unit weight, the strength and yield stress of the blended cement admixed Bangkok clay are practically the same as long as the wc/C value is identical. This ﬁnding is in agreement with that reported for cement admixed Ariake clay by Miura et al. [12]. The wc/C controls not only the strength and the yield stress but also the stress–strain response as shown by the oedometer and the unconﬁned compression test results in Figs. 7 and 8 , respectively. Fig. 7 shows the compression behavior of the blended cement ad- mixed Bangkok clay samples, having the same wc/C value of 12 but with different combinations of water content, cement content and ﬂy ash content after 28 days of curing. The samples were made up from three conditions of clay water content; namely, 89%, 119%, and 148% and three conditions of ﬂy ash content; i.e., 0, 10, and 25. The ( ev, log r0 v ) relationship is plotted so as to take care of the effect of the difference in the initial void ratio. It is found that the yield stress and the deformation behavior at pre-yield stress of all samples, having identical wc/C are practically the same. But the samples with higher clay water content are stable at higher void ratios and provide higher compression indexes beyond yield stress, caused by the destructuring [11–13,33,34]. In other words, the blended cement admixed sample with higher clay water con- tent undergoes higher settlement at post-yield state. Fig. 8 shows the stress–strain relationships in unconﬁned com- pression tests of samples for different initial water contents, cement contents and ﬂy ash contents but the same w c/C values of 4, 7, and 12. It is noted that the lower the wc/C, the stronger the cementation bond, inducing higher strength. The similar Fig. 9. Strength development in the FA blended cement admixed clay. S.
